title: Infant CPR & First Aid — American Red Cross
summary: Every parent and caregiver should know infant CPR before baby arrives. The Red Cross offers in-person and online options — typically a few hours. It's one of those skills you desperately hope never to use and desperately want to have.
body:
The One Thing You Should Do Before Baby Arrives

If you do only one thing from a list of safety preparations, make it infant CPR. The window between a choking or cardiac event and permanent harm is short — minutes matter in a way that almost no other home emergency does. A parent or caregiver who knows what to do, and does it correctly, is the most important variable.

Infant CPR is not the same as adult CPR. The technique is different — the compression depth, hand position, and breathing volumes are all calibrated to a small, fragile body. Watching a YouTube video is better than nothing; taking a class is meaningfully better than watching a video.

What the Class Covers

The American Red Cross Infant CPR and First Aid course typically covers:

Infant CPR: The sequence of checking responsiveness, calling for help, beginning chest compressions, and rescue breaths. The correct hand position for infant compressions (two fingers, below the nipple line), the correct depth (around 4cm / 1.5 inches), the correct rate (100–120 compressions per minute), and the correct ratio (30 compressions to 2 breaths).

Choking response: How to identify when an infant is choking, the back blow and chest thrust technique that dislodges an obstruction in an infant (not the Heimlich manoeuvre, which is used for older children and adults), and when to call for emergency services.

AED use: Basic familiarisation with automated external defibrillators, which are increasingly available in public spaces. Adult pads are not appropriate for infants; the class covers what to do if an AED is available.

Basic first aid: Common infant first aid scenarios — burns, cuts, falls, febrile convulsions — with appropriate responses.

Format Options

In-person classroom. The most valuable format for hands-on technique — you practice compressions on a mannequin, get real-time feedback on depth and rate, and have the chance to ask questions. Typical duration: 4–8 hours. Often offered at hospitals, fire stations, and community centres.

Online with hands-on component. The Red Cross offers a blended option: complete the educational content online at your own pace, then attend a brief in-person skills session (1–2 hours) to practice the techniques. This format is often more convenient and less time-consuming.

Fully online. Available but not recommended as the primary format — the hands-on component is too important for a physical skill. Use it as a refresher after completing an in-person course.

Who Should Take It

Both parents should take the class. Additionally, consider who will regularly care for your baby:

  • Grandparents and extended family who will babysit
  • Nannies and childminders (confirm their certification is current)
  • Siblings old enough to be left alone briefly with a baby

The skill is only useful if the person present knows it. Certification for CPR is typically valid for 2 years.

When to Take It

Aim for the third trimester — close enough to the birth that the technique is recent, early enough that you're not in the frantic last-minute preparation phase. Many hospitals include it in their birth preparation class calendar.

Hospital CPR Classes

Many hospitals offer infant CPR classes specifically for parents expecting at that hospital. Check with your birth facility — these classes are often free or low-cost, are taught by qualified staff, and may include information specific to the hospital's protocols.

Bottom Line

Infant CPR is a short time commitment with potentially life-saving returns. Both parents should be certified before baby arrives. Register for a class in the third trimester — it takes half a day, and having done it provides a quiet peace of mind that's worth the time.
